zotero.abstractNote: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has achieved remarkable success in learning representations from unlabeled data. Despite this, a unified theoretical framework that explains the success of various SSL methods remains elusive. In this work, we propose a probabilistic model that underlies many popular SSL approaches. We show that these methods can be interpreted as performing maximum likelihood estimation of a latent variable model, where the latent variables are the representations. We demonstrate that our framework encompasses several prominent SSL algorithms, including contrastive learning and distillation-based methods. Furthermore, we provide theoretical insights into the role of data augmentation and the choice of architecture in SSL, and we validate our findings through empirical experiments.
